## Granting citizenship: Introduction
A person who is granted citizenship is a naturalized Canadian citizen. Any person granted citizenship is issued documentary evidence of citizenship (certificate of citizenship) in paper or electronic form.
Certain requirements must be met before a person can be granted citizenship. Some of these requirements may be waived by the Minister on compassionate grounds. The Minister also has discretionary authority to grant citizenship.
In most cases, persons 14 years of age or over who are granted citizenship must take the oath of citizenship in order to become citizens. When the oath is required, their legal status as citizens takes effect as of the date the oath is taken. A person who is not required to take the oath becomes a Canadian citizen on the date of the grant decision.
There are different types of grants and different ways to acquire citizenship, as outlined in the Citizenship Act .

### Minor [subsection A5(1)]
Any application made under subsection 5(1) of the Citizenship Act on behalf of a minor must be made by a person who has custody of the minor or who is empowered to act on their behalf.
It is only in cases where the Minister waives this requirement that minors can apply on their own behalf. An authorized person applying on behalf of a minor does not have to be a Canadian citizen.

The authorized person applying on behalf of the minor does not have to be a Canadian citizen. If 14 years of age or older, the minor must countersign the application. Be under 18 years of age at the time of application.

#### Waiving eligibility requirements
The following requirements can be waived in certain circumstances, including on compassionate grounds, for a minor applying under subsection A5(1):
- Having a person who has custody of the minor or who is empowered to act on the minor’s behalf submit an application on behalf of the minor, pursuant to  subparagraph 5(3)(b)(v) of the Citizenship Act.
- Meeting the physical presence requirement, pursuant to subparagraph 5(3)(b)(ii) of the Citizenship Act.
- Taking the oath of citizenship (minors 14 to 17 years of age), pursuant to subparagraph 5(3)(b)(iv) of the Citizenship Act.

### Minor [subsection A5(2)]
A citizenship application for a minor made under subsection A5(2) is processed either concurrently or non-concurrently.
A concurrent application is when a citizenship application for a minor is filed at the same time as application(s) for one or both of the minor’s parents.
A non-concurrent application is when an application is filed on behalf of a minor without it being part of a group or family application that includes one or both parents. In this case, at least one parent is usually already a Canadian citizen or has a citizenship application in process.

#### Eligibility requirements
- Application Submit an application for Canadian citizenship by a parent or guardian. If 14 years of age or older, the minor must countersign the application. Be under 18 years of age at the time of application. Be a child of a Canadian citizen or apply at the same time as a parent who is applying for a citizenship grant.

- Minors over 14 years of age: Individuals 14 years of age and older are legally required to attend the ceremony and take the oath of citizenship, unless the oath has been waived. ### Discretion to waive certain requirements [subsection A5(3)]
Not everyone is able to meet the requirements and conditions for a grant of citizenship under either subsection A5(1) or A5(2); therefore, provisions were made for when individual cases arise where an applicant is unable to meet certain citizenship requirements. Under subsection 5(3) of the Citizenship Act , the Minister has the discretion to waive certain requirements on compassionate grounds.
A waiver under 5(3) of the Citizenship Actis not a type of application; it is an exception to certain requirements for people applying for citizenship under 5(1) or 5(2) of the Citizenship Act.
- Applicants must use the correct application type based on their situation (for example, an adult applicant who is not in the CAF must provide an adult application under subsection A5(1)).
- Applicants are still required to pay the application fees associated with the application type.
- Applicants need to provide the waiver documents that best fit their situation. A web wizard is available on the IRCC website to assist applicants in determining whether they qualify for a waiver and which documents should be submitted with their request.
For an adult applicant (over 18 but under 55 years of age on the date of application), the following requirements can be waived under subsection A5(3):
- the requirement to have adequate knowledge of one of the official languages of Canada; and
- the requirement to demonstrate knowledge of Canada and the responsibilities and privileges of citizenship.
In addition, the requirement to take the oath of citizenship can be waived:
- For adult applicants if they are unable to understand the significance of taking the oath due to mental disability; and
- For minor applicants 14 to 17 years of age, on compassionate grounds.
It is necessary to submit supporting documents to request a waiver (see below for information on supporting documents).
Waivers are not accommodations. If applicants require accommodations, such as access to a mobility device, a sign language interpreter or other, they do not need to request a waiver. Applicants can request accommodations with their application, or they may reach out to IRCC after their application is in process. IRCC may request supporting documentation to meet accommodation requests.

#### Waivers for minors applying under subsection A5(1)
In the case of a minor whose file is being processed under subsection 5(1) of the Citizenship Act, the Minister may waive the requirements respecting
- the length of physical presence in Canada;
- the need to have a parent or person who has custody of or is empowered to act on behalf of the minor apply for them and sign the application; and
- the taking of the oath of citizenship (for applicants 14 years of age and over).

#### Waiver for the language, knowledge or oath-taking requirements
- When an adult applicant under 55 years of age at the time of application has demonstrated to the citizenship officer that they may not be able to meet the language and/or knowledge requirements on compassionate grounds, the applicant should provide a completed Waiver Request form ( CIT 0116 (PDF, 2.17 MB) ) and, if relevant, may also include a Medical Opinion form ( CIT 0547 (PDF, 2.59 MB) ). These forms are available for applicants to provide with their initial applications, but may be submitted once their applications are already in process. Where applicable, the applicant should also submit guardianship documents, power of attorney documents, or an affidavit.
- Adult applicants who require a waiver for the oath-taking requirement should provide a Medical Opinion form ( CIT 0547 (PDF, 2.59 MB) ), completed by a medical professional licensed to practise in Canada. They may choose to provide additional documents to support their request, such as test results or legal documents.
- Minor applicants 14 years of age and over who require a waiver for the oath-taking requirement on compassionate grounds should provide a completed Waiver Request Form for Minors. If the reason they are requesting a waiver is medical in nature, they should provide a Medical Opinion form ( CIT 0547 (PDF, 2.59 MB) ), completed by a medical professional licensed to practise in Canada.
For more information on waivers on compassionate grounds, please see Citizenship: waivers on compassionate grounds .

### Discretionary grants (special cases) [subsection A5(4)]
Subsection 5(4) of the Citizenship Act states: “Despite any other provision of this Act, the Minister may, in his or her discretion, grant citizenship to any person to alleviate cases of statelessness or of special and unusual hardship or to reward services of an exceptional value to Canada.”

#### Eligibility considerations
Grants under this subsection are discretionary and each case is considered on its own merits. This subsection is not to be used as a means of circumventing the normal citizenship process; applicants must demonstrate that their circumstances warrant a discretionary grant of citizenship based on:
- special and unusual hardship; or
- exceptional service to Canada.

#### Application requirements, supporting documentation, and fee requirements
- There is no specific application for a grant under subsection A5(4).
- The applicant submits a citizenship application [usually under subsection A5(1), A11(1) or A5(2)]. An application under subsection A5(5) may also be used.
- On their application, the applicant indicates they would like to be considered for a grant under subsection A5(4) on the basis of supporting documents.
- If IRCC becomes aware of circumstances that merit consideration under subsection A5(4), IRCC may proactively reach out to the client.
- Usually, the fee for the corresponding application should be paid. In some cases, fees may be waived for applications under subsection A5(4).
- The applicant did not voluntarily renounce citizenship in any country.
- The applicant must have attempted to regularize their status in Canada (for example, explored other immigration or refugee protection avenues and been unsuccessful).
- The applicant does not have a concurrent immigration application in process.
- The applicant must be living (be physically present) in Canada at the time of the application.
- If approved, the applicant is required to take the oath of citizenship, unless a waiver has been granted.
For more information on granting citizenship under subsection 5(4) of the Citizenship Act,please see Citizenship: Ministerial discretion to grant citizenship in special cases .

### Statelessness [subsection A5(5)]
This application is for a person who is and has always been stateless, was born outside Canada on or after April 17, 2009, to a  Canadian parent (who was neither born nor naturalized in Canada), and wishes to acquire Canadian citizenship. A person is considered stateless if they are not  recognized as a national by any state under its domestic law.
Citizenship by descent is limited to the first generation born abroad until the end of the invalidity period as a result of the Bjorkquist decision. There are limited exceptions for children and grandchildren of Crown servants who are, at the time of the child’s birth, employed outside Canada by the Canadian Armed Forces, the federal public administration or provincial public service (other than as a locally engaged person). A person who is stateless due to the first-generation limit to citizenship by descent can apply for a grant of citizenship under subsection A5(5).

### Adoption [section A5.1]
The provision for the grant of Canadian citizenship for persons born abroad and adopted by Canadian citizens is set out in section 5.1 of the Citizenship Act.
There are 3 types of adoption situations covered under section 5.1 of the Citizenship Act:
- applicants adopted as minors- A5.1(1);
- applicants adopted as adults- A5.1(2); and
- applicants adopted abroad (minor or adult) and destined to Quebec- A5.1(3).
To make an application for citizenship under the adoptions category, the adoptive parent/guardian applies on behalf of the minor adoptee. If the adoptee is an adult (whether adopted as an adult or minor), they submit the application on their own behalf.

### Automatic acquisition of citizenship [subsection A11(2)] (also considered resumption)
Before January 1, 1947, women lost British subject status:
- by virtue of marriage to a foreigner, or
- by the husband acquiring a foreign nationality during their marriage.
People who lost Canadian citizenship for these reasons may acquire citizenship under subsection 11(2) of the Citizenship Act .
In these circumstances, the applicant cannot acquire citizenship under subsection A11(2) and must submit an Application for a Citizenship Certificate form [ CIT 0001 (PDF, 1.40 MB) ] with applicable documents and fees to obtain a citizenship certificate.

#### Eligibility requirements
- Ceased to be a British subject before January 1, 1947, upon or during marriage.
- Would have been a citizen if the 1947 Act had come into force immediately before her marriage or the acquisition by her husband of a foreign nationality.
Note: The person does not need to meet any prohibition, residence, language or knowledge requirements and is not required to take the oath of citizenship.
There is no application fee.

There is no application form for subsection A11(2).
The applicant must write directly to the Registrar of Canadian Citizenship or send a letter to any citizenship office or mission. Here is an example of a written statement: “I, being a woman who lost British subject status by virtue of my marriage to a non-British subject man, hereby declare my desire to become a Canadian citizen.”

## Grant requirement details

### Physical presence requirement – subparagraph A5(1)(c)(i)
Subparagraph 5(1)(c)(i) of the Citizenship Act sets out the physical presence (PP) requirements for citizenship applications made under subsection A5(1). For applications received on or after October 11, 2017, the applicant must:
- be a permanent resident (PR);
- have no unfulfilled conditions attached to their PR status; and
- have 1,095 days of PP in Canada as a PR (each day an applicant was physically present as an authorized Temporary Resident or Protected Person is credited as a half day up to a maximum of 365 days) during the 5 years immediately before the date of application.
Canadian Armed Forces members need to provide evidence of service instead of meeting the physical presence requirement.

### Canadian Armed Forces – subsections A5(1.2) and A5(1.3)
- A member of the CAF or a foreign military member who is/was attached or seconded to the CAF can apply for citizenship under a  specific application.
- There is a service requirement instead of a physical presence requirement. CAF applicants need to complete 3 years of service in the CAF within the 6 years immediately before applying for citizenship (if the application is signed on/after June 11, 2015). All other requirements under 5(1) of the Citizenship Act must be met, including not being prohibited from becoming a citizen. These cases are processed on a priority basis.
Service in the CAF includes:
- All time spent serving as a member of the CAF (either as a Regular or Reserve Force member), regardless of whether the applicant was paid for full-time service or other than full-time paid service (part-time) or whether the applicant had any period of leave without pay; and
- All time spent serving as a foreign military member attached or seconded to the CAF.

#### Time spent outside Canada as a Crown servant
The exceptions to the physical presence requirement are limited to those who were employed outside Canada by the federal public administration, the public service of a province, the Canadian Armed Forces (CAF), and to those who resided with certain family members who are so employed.
Subsections A5(1.01) to (1.03) describe persons who are eligible to count time spent outside Canada as time in Canada for the purpose of meeting the physical presence requirement.
A5(1.01) - Under subsection 5(1.01) of the Act, a citizenship applicant can count time during which they resided with their Canadian citizen spouse or common-law partner who was employed outside of Canada in or with the CAF, the federal public administration or the public service of a province or territory, otherwise than as a locally engaged person, as time spent residing in Canada for the purpose of meeting the physical presence requirement.
A5(1.02) - Under subsection 5(1.02) of the Act, an applicant can count time outside Canada when they were a PR and were employed outside Canada in or with the CAF, the federal public administration or the public service of a province, otherwise than as a locally engaged person, as time spent residing in Canada for the purpose of meeting the physical presence requirement.
A5(1.03) - Under subsection 5(1.03) of the Act, a citizenship applicant can count time during which they resided with their PR spouse, common-law partner or parent who was employed outside Canada in or with the CAF, the federal public administration or the public service of a province or territory, otherwise than as a locally engaged person, as time spent residing in Canada for the purpose of meeting the physical presence requirement.

### Permanent resident requirement – paragraph A5(1)(c) and subsection A5(2)
Under paragraph 5(1)(c) of the Citizenship Act, applicants must be admitted to Canada as a PR and maintain PR status throughout the qualifying period, up until taking the oath. This provision applies to all applicants who apply under subsection A5(1) on or after June 11, 2015.
As of June 11, 2015, a person cannot be granted citizenship or take the oath of citizenship while still having outstanding terms and conditions on their PR status. This provision applies to all subsection A5(1) applicants who apply on or after June 11, 2015.
The most common terms and conditions include the following:
- PRs under medical surveillance.
The Citizenship Actallows the Minister to suspend an application in certain circumstances, and without time limitation, in order to obtain information needed to make a decision, such as information regarding outstanding terms and conditions.
For more information on outstanding terms and conditions of permanent residence that impact citizenship, please see Citizenship: Conditional permanent residence .

### Income tax requirement – subparagraph A5(1)(c)(iii)
Subsection 5(1) of the Citizenship Act,as amended by Bill C-6, requires citizenship applicants to file an income tax return for 3 of the taxation years (same as calendar years) that fall fully or partially within the 5 years immediately before the date of application, if applicable under the Income Tax Act. This is consistent with the amended requirement that applicants be physically present for 1,095 days (3 years) in the 5 years immediately before the date of application under subparagraph A5(1)(c)(i).
If the applicant declares they were required to file an income tax return, but did not actually do so for at least 3 taxation years, this would be considered non-compliance with the income tax requirement.

### Language requirements – paragraph A5(1)(d)
All subsection A5(1) grant applicants who are at least 18 but under 55 years of age on the date of application are assessed on their ability to communicate in English or French.
Section 14 of the Citizenship Regulations outlines the criteria to be used in determining whether or not an applicant demonstrates an adequate knowledge of English or French pursuant to paragraph A5(1)(d).
IRCC has identified the Canadian Language Benchmark/ Niveau de compétence linguistique canadien (CLB/NCLC) level 4 as the language assessment description that best describes what competencies an applicant for citizenship must demonstrate in order to be considered to have adequate knowledge of one of the official languages. This is meant to ensure that citizens are able to fully participate and integrate into Canadian society.
Achieving a CLB 4 level signifies that an individual is able to:
- take part in short, routine conversations about everyday topics;
- understand simple instructions and directions;
- use basic grammar, including simple structures and tenses, in oral communication; and
- use vocabulary that is adequate for routine oral communication.
Adult citizenship applicants under subsection A5(1) aged 18 to 54 must provide objective evidence of language proficiency by submitting proof of achievement of CLB/NCLC level 4 or higher in speaking and listening with their application. Citizenship officials verify the documents that were submitted and may screen the applicant’s language abilities at program integrity interviews in local offices. The list of language evidence accepted by IRCC includes:
- results of an IRCC-approved third-party language test (only listening and speaking);
- proof of completion of secondary or postsecondary program in French or English, in Canada or abroad; or
- evidence of achieving CLB/NCLC level 4 or higher in certain government-funded language training programs.
Literacy is not a requirement for a grant of citizenship. In this situation, a person must orally demonstrate their knowledge of one of the official languages.
In the case where an applicant is illiterate and is unable to meet the language requirement for this reason, a waiver of the requirement may be granted. Please see Citizenship: waivers on compassionate grounds for more information.

### Knowledge – paragraph A5(1)(e)
The importance of the knowledge requirement is reflected in IRCC’s mission statement. IRCC is committed to building a stronger Canada by supporting the participation and integration of newcomers into Canadian society. Requiring newcomers to demonstrate knowledge of Canada and of the responsibilities and privileges of citizenship in order to become Canadian citizens ensures that new Canadians have the knowledge to facilitate their full integration into society.
All grant applicants under subsection A5(1) who are 18 to 54 years of age must demonstrate, in one of Canada’s official languages, an adequate knowledge of Canada and of the responsibilities and privileges of citizenship.
Section 15 of the Citizenship Regulationsoutlines the criteria to be used in determining whether or not an applicant demonstrates an adequate knowledge of Canada and of the responsibilities and privileges of citizenship. A person is considered to meet this requirement if they demonstrate that they know the national symbols of Canada and have a general understanding of:
- the chief characteristics of Canadian political and military history;
- the chief characteristics of Canadian social and cultural history;
- the chief characteristics of Canadian physical and political geography;
- the chief characteristics of the Canadian system of government as a constitutional monarchy;
- participation in the Canadian democratic process;
- participation in Canadian society (volunteering, respecting the environment, etc.);
- respect for the rights, freedoms and obligations set out in Canada’s laws; and
- characteristics and responsibilities and privileges of citizenship other than those outlined above.
Knowledge of the above criteria is shown by passing a citizenship test.
The citizenship test asks questions about the subject areas elaborated in section 15 of the Citizenship Regulations. All questions are based on the study guide Discover Canada: The Rights and Responsibilities of Citizenship.
Discover Canada: The Rights and Responsibilities of Citizenship is the official study guide that the applicant must study in order to pass the citizenship knowledge test. The study guide covers a variety of topics about Canadian symbols, values, institutions and history, as well as the rights, responsibilities and privileges attached to Canadian citizenship.
The test questions are based on the content of the guide and test the criteria listed in section R15. The guide is available on the IRCC website and is also available in alternative formats such as PDF, e-book, audio, Braille and large print.
In order to pass the written test, citizenship applicants must correctly answer a minimum of 15 out of 20 questions.

### Prohibitions – paragraph A5(1)(f)
The prohibitions sections of the Citizenship Act include sections 20, 21 and 22. Sections A20 and A22 provide the reasons why an applicant cannot be granted citizenship and cannot take the oath, and section A21 outlines the periods of physical presence in Canada that do not count towards citizenship.
Prohibitions are assessed at the date of application, during processing, at the time of decision (grant) and at the time of oath-taking. Assessing prohibitions is not to be delayed until the end of a sentence (jail or probation).
Individuals who are 14 years of age and over are legally required to attend a ceremony and take the oath of citizenship, unless the oath has been waived. Minors have to be 14 years of age and over on the day they take the oath. Their age is not locked in by the application date (signature date). Minors under the age of 14 are not required to attend a ceremony or take the oath, but can choose to attend a ceremony. For applicants required to take the oath, the ceremony occurs after the application is granted.
No oath is required for adoptions under subsection A5(5) or section A5.1.

#### Concurrent minor applications
For concurrent minor applications under subsection A5(2), the ceremony occurs at the same time as the parent’s ceremony. The concurrent minor is granted citizenship after the ceremony. The effective date of citizenship for minors under 14 years of age is the date of the associated parent’s oath-taking. The effective date of citizenship for minors over 14 years of age is the date the minor takes and signs the oath or if the oath has been waived, the date the citizenship officer grants citizenship to the minor (after the parent has taken the oath of citizenship).

#### Non-concurrent minor applications
In the case of a non-concurrent minor application under subsection A5(2), the ceremony occurs after the grant of citizenship. If the minor is under 14 years of age when the application processing is complete, the Case Processing Centre in Sydney (CPC-S) may grant citizenship and send the certificate to the applicant’s last-known address. The effective date of citizenship for these cases is the day that citizenship is granted. If the minor is 14 years of age or older when the application processing is complete, the minor must take the oath. The file is forwarded to the local office. A citizenship officer will grant citizenship once all requirements have been met and will schedule the minor to take the oath at a citizenship ceremony. The effective date of citizenship for these cases is the day the oath of citizenship is taken by the minor.

### Application fees – paragraph A13(c)
Fees depend on whether the applicant is an adult (aged 18 and over) or a minor (under 18 years of age). Fees may include:
- the processing fee; and
- the right of citizenship fee.
Only adult applicants pay the right of citizenship fee.
Fees must be paid online.
- Fees are paid once the applicant is ready to submit the application.
- The fee receipt must be included with the application.
- If a group or family application is submitted, all fees can be paid together (one receipt) or separately (multiple receipts).
For more information, see Pay your fees online (opens in a new tab) .

### Group or family applications
- To be considered a group or family application, all applications must be complete.
- All applications, documents and receipts are placed in the same envelope. IRCC will process all applications in the same envelope together. If applications are not in the same envelope, IRCC will process them separately.
- IRCC will process all applications in the same envelope together.
- If applications are not in the same envelope, IRCC will process them separately.
In an online account, applications can be added or other accounts can be invited to join the group or family.

#### Incomplete applications
If one or more of the applications are missing necessary information or documents and must be returned, all applications will be returned.
Note: It is not possible for multiple applicants to be on one citizenship application; each applicant must submit their own application for citizenship. Applications are processed in a group when several applications are submitted together in one envelope.
IRCC can only process citizenship applications urgently in special cases or emergencies. IRCC will ensure every urgent request meets the conditions for urgent processing. If the application does not meet the conditions, it will be processed normally.
To apply for urgent processing, applicants must do the following:
- Mailed in paper applications: Write “Urgent” in dark letters on the envelope, provide an explanation for why urgent processing is required and provide documents to support the urgent request.
- Electronic applications submitted online: Attach a document explaining why urgent processing is required and provide documents to support the urgent request.
Examples of special cases or emergencies:
- a requirement for Canadian citizenship in order to: apply for a job; avoid losing a current job; or attend to a Canadian school, college or university.
- avoid losing a current job; or
- attend to a Canadian school, college or university.
- necessity to travel because of death or serious illness in the group or family, and the applicant is unable to obtain a passport in their current nationality.
- receiving a successful Federal Court decision on an appeal on a previous application for citizenship.
There is no guarantee that the request for urgent processing will be granted, even if the conditions are met. IRCC may not be able to process the application in time to remedy the situation.
If the applicant has already sent their application and needs urgent processing, they can contact IRCC using the Web Form or reach out to the Client Support Centre.The applicant must explain why urgent processing is required and submit documents to support their request.

### Using a representative
A representative can be:
- an immigration or citizenship consultant.
They can be paid or unpaid. If paid , they must be a member in good standing of a law society or governing body .
- Citizenship or immigration consultants must be members of the College of Immigration and Citizenship Consultants .
- Lawyers or notaries must be members of a Canadian provincial or territorial law society , or the Chambre des notaires du Québec .
- Paralegals (Ontario only) must be members of the Law Society of Ontario .
After the application to IRCC is submitted, IRCC will contact the representative (not the applicant) for all updates on the application.
- The representative can tell the applicant about any updates.
- The representative can forward any invitations or letters.
- IRCC will send all correspondence and communications to the representative.
The applicant does not have to get help from another person or a representative to apply. It is their choice.
The applicant does not have to submit (complete) an IMM 5476 if the representative helps them to fill out the application but does not want to conduct business on their behalf after they submit it. IRCC will contact the applicant directly.
If the applicant wants to appoint a representative to communicate and conduct business with IRCC on their behalf, they must fill out and submit the latest version of the Use of a Representative form ( IMM 5476 (PDF, 268 KB) ). The applicant is still responsible for the accuracy of the information provided to IRCC.
For further information on representatives, please refer to the website .
IRCC has a duty to accommodate applicants when required and/or requested by the applicant.
Accommodations are used to enable applicants to meet the requirements for citizenship through a lens of equity. They can include:
- ensuring any in-person event venues have accessibility for wheelchairs and service animals, and that interpretation services are arranged.
- providing alternate versions of the study guide, oral or Braille versions of the citizenship knowledge test, or extra time on tests.
